dialogues.text: In the Warmup, you saw equal groups lined up into a rectangle. See this sticker sheet? The stickers aren't bunched up in bags or scattered around. They're lined up. Every sticker has a spot. Let me show you what I notice.
See how the stickers are lined up? They go across, and they go up and down. Everything lines up into a rectangle.
When objects are arranged like this, lined up in a rectangle, with every spot filled, that's called an ARRAY.
Now let me teach you the names for what you're seeing. Watch this.
See this line going across? That's a row. Rows go across from left to right.
Row 1, row 2, row 3. Three rows, and they all go across.
Now watch this direction. See this line going up and down? That's a column. Columns go up and down.
Column 1, column 2, column 3, column 4. Four columns, all going up and down.
So think about the difference between rows and columns. How many rows that go across does this array have?
3 rows — the lines that go across. So this array has 3 rows and 4 columns. An array is equal groups organized into rows and columns.
Here's a new array, this time with dots. How many rows does this array have? Remember, rows go across.
2 rows. The lines going across.
Now how many columns? Columns go up and down.
6 columns. The lines going up and down. So this array has 2 rows and 6 columns.
This array has 3 rows, and each row has 5 dots. So we can say: 3 rows of 5.
Here's how that becomes a multiplication expression. Watch.
3 rows of 5. The first number is how many rows: that's 3.
The second number is how many in each row: that's 5.
3 rows of 5. That's just like saying 3 groups of 5, and you know equal groups means multiplication. 3 rows of 5 becomes 3 times 5.
Now here's a new array.
Build the expression. The first number is how many rows, the second is how many in each row.
4 times 2. 4 rows of 2. The words match the expression.
One more. Look at this array and build the expression for the rows.
2 times 5. 2 rows of 5. You've got it.
So far, you've been describing arrays by rows, the lines going across. But here's something interesting about arrays: there's another way to describe them.
You just described this array by ROWS: 2 rows of 5. That gave us 2 times 5.
But watch what happens when I look at the COLUMNS instead.
I see 5 columns. Each column has 2 dots. So I can also say: 5 columns of 2.
5 columns of 2 becomes 5 times 2. The first number is how many columns. The second number is how many in each column.
Same array, but a different description which gives us a different expression. 2 rows of 5 gave us 2 times 5. 5 columns of 2 gives us 5 times 2. Both describe THIS array.
The rule is: your DESCRIPTION determines your EXPRESSION. What you SAY about the array is what you WRITE. Let's try one.
This array has 4 rows of 3. But what's the column description? Count the columns going up and down, then count how many in each column.
3 columns of 4. Now build the expression to match.
3 columns of 4. 3 times 4. The description matches the expression.
Check out this array. First, describe it by rows. How many rows, and how many in each row? Build the row expression.
4 times 5 — 4 rows of 5.
Same array. Now describe it by columns: how many columns, how many in each column? Build the column expression.
5 times 4 — 5 columns of 4. Same array, two different expressions.
Look at these two pictures. On the left, an ice cube tray. On the right, a dot array. Same number of rows. Same number of columns. Whether it's ice cubes or dots, it's the same array. 2 rows of 6.
The math is the same no matter what fills the spots. Build the row expression.
2 times 6. Whether it's ice cubes or dots, the math is the same.
Here's your chance to choose. You can describe this array by rows OR by columns. It's up to you. Then build the full equation, including the product.
First, choose whether you'd like to describe this array by rows or columns.
Now describe this array by words and build the equation.
You described it by rows. 3 rows of 4. 3 times 4 equals 12.
Now describe this array by words and build the equation.
You described it by columns. 4 columns of 3. 4 times 3 equals 12.

You can describe arrays by rows or by columns, and each description gives you a different expression. Now you're going to BUILD arrays yourself. Tell the computer what to make, and then find the answer.
Now YOU get to build arrays. Watch how this works.
I want to make 3 rows of 5. I said ROWS first, so I build the rows first.
I add my first row.
Second row.
Third row. I've set up 3 rows but there are no dots yet. I need to add columns to fill them in.
Now I add items to each row. Watch what happens. Each + puts one dot in every row.
One dot in each row. That's 3 dots.
Two dots in each row, 6 dots. Keep going.
3, 4, 5 in each row. 3 rows of 5. 15 dots. I built it.
Now I write the equation.
3 rows of 5. 3 times 5 equals 15. I planned it, built it, and found the answer. Your turn.
Build an array with 4 rows of 6. Start with the rows.
4 rows. Now add the items into every row.
4 rows of 6. You built it.
Now write the equation for 4 rows of 6.
4 times 6 equals 24. You built the array and found the product.
Now build by columns first. Make 2 columns of 5.
2 columns. Now add the items in each column. Each plus puts one dot in every column.
2 columns of 5. You built it column by column.
Now write the equation for 2 columns of 5.
2 times 5 equals 10. Building by columns works the same way. The description matches the expression.
Last challenge. I want an array with 20 dots total. You choose: build it with rows first or columns first, pick your numbers, and find the equation. There's more than one right answer.
You built it. Now write the word description for your array.
Now write the equation. What multiplication does your array show?
4 times 5 equals 20. You chose 4 rows of 5. That's one way to build 20.
You've learned to describe arrays by rows or columns, write expressions for each description, and find products. Let's see what you know.
